summary:
The Ratification of the United States Constitution (1787–1788 AD) was the founding of the world's oldest surviving written constitutional republic, establishing a governmental framework of separated powers and federalism that has endured for over two centuries and profoundly influenced constitutional design worldwide. The Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia (May–September 1787), convened because the [[event:3313|Articles of Confederation]] had proven inadequate for governing the new nation, produced a document of extraordinary intellectual sophistication: 55 delegates (including Washington, Madison, Hamilton, Franklin, and James Wilson) spent four months in secret deliberations, producing a constitution that balanced competing interests — large vs. small states (the Great Compromise creating a bicameral Congress), slave-holding vs. free states (the three-fifths compromise, the fugitive slave clause), and national vs. state sovereignty (federalism).

The [[event:3314|Federalist Papers]] (1787–1788), written by Madison, Hamilton, and Jay to support ratification in New York, provided the most sophisticated theoretical defense of the Constitution's design — particularly Madison's argument in Federalist No. 10 that a large republic would better control factionalism than a small one (reversing conventional wisdom), and Federalist No. 51's argument for separated powers as mutual checks on tyranny: 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.' Ratification was contested; the Anti-Federalists demanded a Bill of Rights, which Madison drafted and Congress proposed in 1789 (ratified 1791). The Constitution's innovations — judicial review, federal supremacy in enumerated powers, an independent executive presidency, a bicameral legislature with direct popular representation — created a template imitated in varying degrees by constitutional framers in France (1791), Latin America, and ultimately most of the world's democracies.
